1.18: A direct revelation: 'The word of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God.' Paul's theologia crucis (theology of the cross) is the irreducible Christological claim: God's power is revealed not in imperial might or philosophical sophistry but in the crucified Christ. The cross does not merely illustrate a timeless spiritual principle — it is the specific historical event in which God's wisdom and power are concentrated and disclosed. Every Corinthian pretension to superior wisdom is measured against this standard: does it stand or fall before the cross?
1.30: A direct revelation: 'He is the source of your life in Christ Jesus, whom God made our wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.' Christ as the personified Wisdom of God (cf. Prov 8:22-31; Sir 24; Wis 7:22-8:1) answers Corinthian competition over human wisdom with a Christological absorbing of all wisdom into one person. The four nouns (wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, redemption) are not merely metaphors but ontological claims: in Christ, believers receive these realities as gifts, not achievements. This is the Christological inversion of the Torah: where Torah promised blessing through obedience, Christ provides the reality the Torah pointed toward.
1.1: The theme: Paul as called apostle of Christ Jesus by God's will. The opening places Christ at the center of the letter's authority structure: Paul's apostleship is a commission from Christ, not a self-appointed role. Every instruction and rebuke in the letter derives its authority from this Christological ground. The NT pattern of divine calling through Christ recapitulates the OT prophet's commissioning: called not by human agency but by divine appointment.
1.2: The church is defined by two Christological realities: sanctified en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus) and called to be holy people. Holiness in the OT was participation in YHWH's character (Lev 11:44-45); in Paul, holiness is participation in Christ's own status before God — the sanctification Christ accomplished is the ground of the community's identity as holy. The letter is addressed to all who call on the name of the Lord — the Shema-style confession now directed to Jesus Christ as Lord.
1.3: The blessing formula places Christ alongside the Father as the source of grace and peace. The standard Jewish greeting adapted by Paul identifies both God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as the shared origin of the community's blessing — a Trinitarian structure embedded in every Pauline letter-opening. The co-ordination of Father and Christ as joint source is an implicit Christological claim about Christ's divine status.
1.4: The grace given in Christ Jesus is the Christological ground of all thanksgiving. The gifts the Corinthians have received are not generic divine endowments but specifically located en Christō Iēsou (in Christ Jesus) — within the sphere of Christ's accomplishment. Gratitude directed toward God for gifts received in Christ creates the anti-boasting logic that will govern the entire letter.
1.5: Enriched in all speech and knowledge en autō (in him): the gifts of eloquence and understanding are Christ-given, not self-generated achievements. This direct address to the Corinthians' prized capacities establishes them as Christological gifts before critiquing their misuse. Gifts given by Christ cannot be grounds for competition between Christ's people.
1.6: The testimony of Christ was confirmed among them — Paul's gospel proclamation about Christ was not his own biography but a witness to what Christ had done. The divine confirmation of this testimony (through signs and transformed lives, cf. 2:4) is Christ's own authentication of the message about himself. The community's existence is the confirmation of Christ's testimony.
1.7: The gifts flow from the expectation of Christ's revelation (apokalypsin tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou): the community waits for the unveiling of the Lord Jesus Christ at the Parousia. All spiritual gifts are transitional provisions for the period between resurrection and return — they point toward and will be superseded by Christ's full manifestation. Christological eschatology is the frame within which the letter's discussion of gifts (chs 12-14) must be read.
1.8: Christ will keep them firm to the end so they will be blameless on his Day. The Day of the Lord, now recast as the Day of our Lord Jesus Christ, is the eschatological horizon that gives Paul's ethical instructions their urgency. Blamelessness on that Day is not achieved by human effort but guaranteed by Christ's own faithfulness: he will sustain what he began.
1.9: The faithfulness of God is expressed through the call into koinōnia with his Son — fellowship, participation, sharing. This is the deepest Christological claim in the letter's opening: the community's life is not merely association with Christ but participation in him. The whole of 1 Corinthians is an argument that this participation in Christ must reshape every sphere of community life: factions, sexuality, food, worship, resurrection hope.
1.10: The appeal for unity is made through the name of the Lord Jesus Christ — that name carries the authority and identity of Christ himself as the community's single Lord. Division in the community is a fracturing of Christ's own body (v. 13: is Christ divided?). The unity Paul seeks is not merely social harmony but a Christological necessity: there is one Christ, therefore there must be one community.
1.11: The report of quarrels (eris) comes from Chloe's household. The factions represent a fundamental misunderstanding of what Christ accomplished: if different apostles are merely channels through whom one Christ operates, there is no basis for choosing between them. The quarrels reveal a misplaced Christology — treating apostolic personalities as the ultimate referent instead of Christ himself.
1.12: The party-names (I follow Paul; I follow Apollos; I follow Cephas; I follow Christ) all reveal the same error: making Christ one option among several allegiances. Even the Christ-party, if it understood itself as superior to the others, was using Christ as a faction-banner rather than recognizing him as the Lord of all the parties. The use of Christ's name to build a competitive faction is a misappropriation of his person.
1.13: Three rhetorical questions expose the absurdity of the factions: Is Christ divided? — No, his body is one. Was Paul crucified for you? — No, only Christ was crucified for sinners. Were you baptized into Paul's name? — No, baptism creates union with Christ, not with apostles. All three questions place the saving events — cross and baptism — in Christ alone, not in any apostolic figure. Christology demolishes the factional logic.
1.14: Paul's relief at not having baptized many in Corinth is grounded in the exclusive Christological allegiance that baptism creates. Baptism in the NT is eis Christon (into Christ), not into an apostle's sphere of influence. The factional problem is partly liturgical: baptism wrongly understood creates patron-client bonds with the baptizer rather than covenant-union with Christ.
1.15: The concern is that no one could say they were baptized into Paul's name — the name of baptism belongs to Christ alone. The threefold baptismal name (Matt 28:19: Father, Son, Holy Spirit) or the simple name of the Lord Jesus Christ (Acts 2:38) establishes Christ as the one into whose reality believers enter at baptism. No apostle can share that exclusive position.
1.16: The parenthetical admission about Stephanas's household does not undermine the point: Paul is not denying that he baptized, but that he baptized in his own name or to create personal followings. Christological allegiance through baptism is to Christ; the agent of baptism is irrelevant to the union created.
1.17: The cross must not be emptied (kenōthē ho stauros) by the wisdom of words: rhetorical display draws attention to the speaker and away from the saving event. The cross is the singular Christological fact; everything else serves to make it visible or obscures it. The tension between eloquent proclamation and cross-centred content runs through all Pauline preaching.
1.19: Isa 29:14 — God will destroy the wisdom of the wise — is read as prophetic anticipation of the cross. The same divine logic that judged Israel's trust in human wisdom now operates through the crucified Christ: God's wisdom-demolishing wisdom is concentrated in the foolishness of the cross. The prophetic judgment on human self-reliance finds its ultimate expression in the cross event.
1.20: The cross has made obsolete the categories of present-age wisdom: the philosopher, the scribe, the rhetor have no answer to the cross. Christ crucified is not a proposition that can be evaluated by the world's standards because it belongs to the coming age breaking into the present — the age whose wisdom the cross inaugurates. The cross renders the wise people of this age (ho aion houtos) without a valid framework.
1.21: God chose to save through the foolishness of proclamation: the proclaimed Christ-event is the vehicle of salvation that natural wisdom cannot replicate. The wisdom of God deployed in the cross is not a higher grade of the same rational process but a completely different epistemological mode — revelation through historical event and Spirit-enabled reception, not philosophical demonstration. Christology is the content of the foolish proclamation that saves.
1.22: Jews seek signs and Greeks seek wisdom: both are seeking validation in their own preferred categories. Christ crucified satisfies neither demand on its own terms — he is not the sign of military victory Jews hoped for, nor the philosophical sage Greeks admired. He is something categorically different: the wisdom and power of God in the form of a crucified criminal, legible only from within the community shaped by the Spirit.
1.23: We preach Christ crucified: Paul's singular content. A crucified Messiah was a contradiction in Jewish expectation (Messiah = triumphant Davidic king) and a scandal in Greek thought (the divine cannot suffer shame). The cross is precisely this offense — it refuses to be domesticated into either tradition's pre-existing categories. This is why Christ crucified requires new categories: the cross creates the epistemological conditions for understanding it.
1.24: Christ as the power of God and the wisdom of God: the two titles absorb what Jews and Greeks respectively sought. They were right to seek power and wisdom — they were wrong about their sources and forms. Christ satisfies both quests at the level of ultimate fulfillment: God's own power (not Roman imperial power) and God's own wisdom (not Sophistic eloquence) are both incarnated and crucified in Christ.
1.25: The foolishness of God is wiser than human wisdom; the weakness of God is stronger than human strength: the paradox is not resolved but intensified. This is not saying that the cross turns out to be secretly strong after all by human standards — it remains genuinely weak by those standards. The point is that divine categories transcend human ones: God's apparent foolishness and weakness exceed all human maxima. Christ crucified is not a halfway-house between wisdom and folly but an entirely different axis.
1.26: Not many wise, powerful, or noble were called: the social composition of the community is itself a Christological statement. God's election operates through the cross-pattern — choosing what the world despises. The same wisdom that chose the cross chose the weak and lowly as the primary recipients. The community's social profile is an embodied argument for the cross's logic.
1.27: God chose the foolish things to shame the wise, the weak things to shame the strong. This divine reversal pattern runs from the OT through the beatitudes (Matt 5:3-12) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) and finds its concentrated expression in the cross: the King of Israel dies as a criminal, the strong one is crucified in weakness. The community's existence as the foolish-and-weak-chosen continues the cross-pattern into the social world.
1.28: The lowly, despised, and non-existent things chosen by God to nullify things that are: the cross is God's act of new creation from nothing. As God created from non-being in Gen 1:1, so in the cross God creates new covenant-people from those who were nobodies. The eschatological community is the creation-from-nothing that the cross enacts — the ultimate demonstration that salvation is entirely God's act, not human achievement.
1.29: So that no flesh may boast before God: the anti-boasting purpose of divine election through weakness and the cross. Boasting (kauchēsis) in Paul is always displaced by the cross: anyone who genuinely encounters the crucified Christ has no standing to claim achievement-credit. The cross does not merely lower human pride — it eliminates the ontological ground for it.
1.31: Let the one who boasts boast in the Lord (Jer 9:24): the only legitimate boast is one directed toward God — specifically, knowing God through Christ. In the NT context, boasting in the Lord means boasting in the crucified and risen Christ, in whom all divine gifts are given. Christological doxology is the redeemed form of human pride: glory directed entirely toward the God revealed in Christ.

5.7: A fulfillment: 'Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed.' Pascha hemon etythē Christos — Paul's most explicit identification of Christ with the Passover sacrifice. The Passover lamb (Exod 12) was killed at the appointed time, its blood placed on doorposts, its eating a communal participation in deliverance. Paul applies all three registers to Christ: appointed time (the cross at Passover), blood as protection, and communal participation (the Lord's Supper as new Passover meal). The command 'let us celebrate the festival' (v. 8) invites believers into ongoing Christological Passover-living: sincerity and truth as the unleavened bread that accompanies the feast.
5.1: The sexual immorality Paul confronts belongs to the broader pattern of the church as new covenant community under Christ's holiness. The sin mirrors the defilement that led to exile in the OT — the community's tolerance of it mirrors Israel's tolerance of covenant-breaking that provoked divine judgment.
5.2: The community's arrogance (phusiomenoi) while harboring open sin mirrors the contradiction of Pharisaic honor-before-God alongside moral failure that Jesus exposed (Matt 23:27-28). A community boasting in Christ while sheltering unrepentant sin is a community that has lost the logic of the cross.
5.3: Paul pronounces judgment in the name of the Lord Jesus — the judicial authority Paul exercises is Christ's own authority delegated to the apostle (Matt 18:18-20; John 20:23). Paul's absence in body but presence in spirit echoes Christ's promised presence when two or three gather in his name.
5.4: When you are assembled in the name of the Lord Jesus with the power of the Lord Jesus: the gathering of the congregation in Christ's name is the locus of his judicial authority. The assembly is not a mere human meeting but a gathering under the kingly rule of the risen Lord.
5.5: Handed over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord — the death-and-resurrection pattern: destruction of the flesh in the present age is the path to salvation in the eschatological day. This mirrors the logic of the cross: through death, life. The day of the Lord is the day of Christ's return and final judgment.
5.6: A little leaven leavens the whole lump — the leaven metaphor connects directly to the Passover context of v.7-8. In the Passover preparations, all leaven was removed from the household before the festival (Exod 12:15). The community is the household; sin is the leaven that must be removed before the feast can be kept.
5.8: Let us keep the festival not with the old leaven, the leaven of malice and evil, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth — the Passover feast is now the ongoing life of the Christian community. Since Christ the Passover lamb has been sacrificed (v.7), the entire life of the church becomes a perpetual Passover feast, kept pure by the removal of moral leaven.
5.9: Paul's earlier letter about not associating with the sexually immoral — the instruction to maintain the holiness of the community follows from Christ's own teaching on binding and loosing (Matt 18:15-20) and the separation of the holy community from defilement.
5.10: Not meaning the immoral of this world — Paul clarifies: separation from the immoral within the community, not withdrawal from the world. This echoes Jesus's prayer in John 17:15-16: not that they be taken out of the world, but that they be kept from the evil one. The church is in the world but not of it.
5.11: Not to associate with one called a brother who is sexually immoral: the category of brother (adelphos) — one who shares the same Father in Christ — creates a higher standard of accountability than applies to outsiders. The covenant community of the new Exodus holds a holiness commensurate with the sacrifice that constituted it.
5.12: Is it not those inside the church whom you are to judge? — the church's internal discipline reflects Christ's own shepherding of his flock. The shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to seek the one (Luke 15:4-7) exercises this same internal care for each member of the community.
5.13: God will judge those outside — Paul transfers final judgment of outsiders to God alone, echoing Jesus's teaching: do not judge, lest you be judged (Matt 7:1). The church's discipline is remedial and restorative; God's judgment of the world is eschatological and final.

10.4: A type: 'They drank from the spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Christ.' Paul applies a midrashic tradition (the rock of Exod 17:6 and Num 20:11 that followed Israel in the wilderness — attested in various Second Temple sources) and identifies it typologically with Christ. The wilderness generation had Christological benefits before the incarnation: they ate the spiritual food (manna) and drank the spiritual drink (water from the rock) that pointed forward to and derived its sustaining power from Christ. Christ was the substance that every wilderness provision shadowed.
10.1: 'Our fathers were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea.' The exodus generation is claimed as corporate ancestor ('our fathers') by Paul — a Gentile-majority community in Corinth. The church, as the new Israel, inherits the typological history of Israel: the exodus story is not alien background but the community's own story, reread through Christ. The cloud (divine presence and guidance) and the sea (the crossing that created the nation) are the foundational saving events that find their antitype in the Spirit and baptism.
10.2: 'All were baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea.' The typological identification of the sea-crossing with baptism uses the same preposition ('into Moses') as 'baptized into Christ' (Rom 6:3; Gal 3:27). Moses as the mediator-representative whom the people were united with typifies Christ — the greater mediator into whom believers are baptized. The cloud as divine Spirit-presence (the pillar of cloud that guided Israel, Exod 13:21-22) also types the Spirit given at Christian baptism. The entire exodus becomes a Christological-pneumatological type.
10.3: 'All ate the same spiritual food.' The manna that God provided daily in the wilderness (Exod 16) is called 'spiritual food' (pneumatikon broma) — not because it was immaterial but because it was supernaturally given and pointed beyond itself to the true bread from heaven. Jesus in John 6:31-35 explicitly identifies the manna as a type of himself ('I am the bread of life... not like the bread the fathers ate'). The wilderness feeding was a sacramental sign of the Christ who is the ultimate sustainer of God's people.
10.5: 'Nevertheless, with most of them God was not pleased, for they were overthrown in the wilderness.' Despite having all the typological benefits — the Christological cloud, sea-baptism, spiritual food, and spiritual drink — the wilderness generation perished. The warning is stark: sacramental participation does not guarantee salvation. The Corinthians who received baptism and ate the Lord's Supper could not rest on these as automatic guarantees of standing before God, any more than the wilderness generation could rest on the exodus.
10.6: 'Now these things took place as examples (typoi) for us, that we might not desire evil as they did.' Paul explicitly names the hermeneutical principle: the wilderness events were typoi — patterns, prefigurations written into Israel's history with the new covenant community in view. The typological reading is not allegorization but a claim about God's providential structuring of Israel's history: the wilderness generation enacted patterns of failure that the Christ-community must avoid. Desire for evil was Israel's root failure; the same root still threatens the church.
10.7: 'Do not be idolaters as some of them were; as it is written, 'The people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play.'' The golden calf episode (Exod 32:6) — Israel's great apostasy from covenant loyalty at the very moment of Moses's mediatorial absence receiving the Torah — provides the type for Corinthian compromise with idol-food culture. The eating and drinking 'at play' (paizein) at the golden calf parallels the Corinthian temple banquets. The Christ who has established the new covenant is dishonored when his people participate in the worship of rivals.
10.8: 'We must not indulge in sexual immorality as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in a single day.' The reference is to the Baal Peor incident (Num 25:1-9) where Israel joined in the sexual cult associated with Moabite worship. The Christological application: Christ as the divine husband of Israel (and now the church) is jealous for his people's exclusive covenant loyalty. Sexual immorality in the Corinthian context (temple prostitution as part of the Aphrodite cult) was not merely personal sin but covenantal adultery against the Lord who redeemed them.
10.9: 'We must not put Christ to the test, as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents.' The 'testing' at Massah-Meribah (Num 21:4-6; cf. Ps 95:8-9) where Israel complained against God's provision is applied to 'Christ' — a striking theological claim. Paul assumes Christ's divine pre-existence: the 'Lord' who was tested in the wilderness is the pre-incarnate Christ (connecting to v. 4: 'the rock was Christ'). The Corinthians who tested God's patience with their eucharistic abuses were testing Christ himself.
10.10: 'Nor grumble, as some of them did and were destroyed by the Destroyer.' The wilderness murmuring traditions (Num 11:1; 14:2; 16:41) are warnings for the Corinthian community whose divisions (1:10-12) and complaints about Paul (4:18-19) echo the wilderness pattern. The 'Destroyer' (olothreutēs) evokes the destroyer angel of the Passover (Exod 12:23) and the judgment at Korah's rebellion (Num 16:41-50). Christ who delivered Israel from the destroying angel is also the judge who exercises judgment through discipline (v. 22).
10.11: 'Now these things happened to them as an example, but they were written down for our instruction, on whom the end of the ages has come.' The Christological hermeneutic is explicit: the OT was written for us, the community living at 'the ends of the ages' (ta telē tōn aiōnōn). Paul locates the church at the eschatological junction where all prior history finds its goal. The wilderness generation's stories are not museum pieces but active instruction for those living in the final, decisive act of God's redemptive drama inaugurated in Christ's death and resurrection.
10.12: 'Let anyone who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall.' The warning is addressed to the spiritually self-confident — those who, like the wilderness generation, assume that covenant status guarantees security. The Corinthians who possessed spiritual gifts, baptism, and eucharistic participation were tempted to presumption. The humility this verse calls for is shaped by Christological realism: standing is a position maintained by the faithfulness of the one who holds us, not by our own achieved security.
10.13: 'God is faithful, and he will not let you be tempted beyond your ability, but with the temptation he will also provide the way of escape (ekbasin), that you may be able to endure it.' The faithfulness of God is the Christological foundation of moral endurance. Christ himself was 'tempted in every way as we are, yet without sin' (Heb 4:15) — in his wilderness temptation (Matt 4:1-11) he passed where Israel failed and blazed the trail that becomes the ekbasis for those who are in him. The way of escape is not evasion but Christ-shaped endurance.
10.14: 'Therefore, my beloved, flee from idolatry.' The 'therefore' (dioper) — consequential command — follows from the assurance of v. 13: because God provides escape, flee. The command to flee (pheugete) is urgent and physical — not 'reconsider your theological framework about idols' but 'get out.' The Corinthian practice of attending temple banquets as a matter of social and commercial necessity is incompatible with the exclusive loyalty that Christ requires of those who are bought with his blood (6:20).
10.15: 'I speak as to sensible people; judge for yourselves what I say.' Paul's appeal to the Corinthians' own judgment (ironically, given his critique of their wisdom-pride) about the eucharistic theology he is about to articulate signals confidence in the argument's self-evidence. Those who have received the Spirit — and the Corinthians prided themselves on spiritual gifts — should be able to discern the incompatibility between the Lord's table and idols' tables. The appeal to wisdom serves the Christological argument: Christ's presence at the table is real enough to make competing allegiances dangerous.
10.16: 'The cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation (koinōnia) in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ?' The eucharistic theology is unambiguous: the Lord's Supper is genuine koinōnia (fellowship, participation, sharing) in the blood and body of Christ. Koinōnia is not merely emotional association but real participation — the same word used for partnership in business and in the gospel (Phil 1:5). The broken bread and poured cup make present the once-for-all sacrifice and effect real participation in its saving benefits.
10.17: 'Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all partake of the one bread.' The unity of the church derives from the unity of Christ: many members, one bread, one body. The church's unity is not achieved through organizational effort or doctrinal consensus but through Eucharistic participation in the one Christ. The 'one body' here anticipates the body-of-Christ ecclesiology of ch. 12: the church's corporate identity is constituted by the one Christ who is both the bread distributed and the body formed by its reception.
10.18: 'Consider the people of Israel: are not those who eat the sacrifices participants in the altar?' The Levitical sacrificial system provides the type: eating the portion of the sacrifice that was not burned created solidarity with the altar and with the deity to whom it was offered. The priest and worshiper who ate the peace offering (Lev 7:15-18) were participants in the covenant relationship the sacrifice maintained. Paul uses this participatory logic to argue that eating idol-food at temple banquets creates an analogous — and incompatible — participation with what is sacrificed there.
10.19: 'What do I imply then? That food offered to idols is anything, or that an idol is anything?' Paul's careful qualification prevents misreading: idols are ontologically nothing (as he argued in 8:4-6 — 'there is no God but one'). The problem is not that idol-food has been spiritually contaminated but that eating at the idol's table creates a social and spiritual solidarity with demonic power. The distinction between the ontological status of idols (nothing) and the social/spiritual reality of idol-worship (participation in the demonic) is the key move.
10.20: 'No, I imply that what pagans sacrifice they offer to demons and not to God.' Paul draws on the Deuteronomy 32:17 tradition ('they sacrificed to demons, not to God') to identify the spiritual reality behind pagan worship: the powers behind the idols are demonic. The idol itself is nothing, but the cult surrounding it is a demonic system. Participation in that system — even incidentally through table fellowship — creates an incompatible allegiance with the Lord whose table the Corinthians also eat at. The demonic and the divine cannot share a patron.
10.21: 'You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons.' The absolute incompatibility of the two tables is a Christological claim: Christ's lordship is exclusive. The 'Lord's table' is not merely a communal meal but the place of Christ's real presence — where his body and blood are participated in (v. 16). Divided table loyalty is spiritual adultery: the covenant Lord who bought the community with his blood will not share his people with the demonic powers they serve at pagan shrines.
10.22: 'Shall we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he?' The divine jealousy language comes from Deuteronomy 32:21 — the Song of Moses which describes Israel's apostasy provoking God's jealous wrath. Paul applies this to the Corinthians: they are replaying Israel's wilderness apostasy (the golden calf's eating and drinking, v. 7) by their divided table loyalty. The rhetorical question 'are we stronger than he?' exposes the absurdity of idolatry — choosing the weaker power (demons) over the stronger (the Lord who raises the dead).
10.23: 'All things are lawful, but not all things are helpful. All things are lawful, but not all things build up.' The libertine slogan ('all things are lawful') that apparently circulated in Corinth (cf. 6:12) is conceded in principle but immediately qualified by the Christological criterion of love: what builds up the community of Christ is the standard, not what the individual's freedom licenses. The freedom Christ won is freedom for love, not freedom from accountability to the body of Christ.
10.24: 'Let no one seek his own good, but the good of his neighbor.' The neighbor-love principle — Lev 19:18, cited by Jesus as the summary of the law alongside love for God (Mark 12:31) — becomes the operative principle for resolving the idol-food question. The 'strong' who have knowledge that idols are nothing must not exercise their knowledge at the expense of the 'weak' whose conscience is damaged by seeing believers eat idol-food. Christ's cross is the ultimate expression of seeking the other's good at the cost of one's own rights.
10.25: 'Eat whatever is sold in the meat market without raising any question on the ground of conscience.' The practical guideline for daily life in a pagan city: do not scruple about meat purchased in the market, since its prior cultic history is irrelevant once it enters normal commercial circulation. The 'earth is the Lord's' (v. 26) means that all created food belongs to God and is available for his people's use — the created world is not under demonic ownership by default. This is the Christological affirmation of creation's goodness despite its contamination by sin.
10.26: 'For the earth is the Lord's, and everything in it' — a direct citation of Psalm 24:1. The psalm is about the Lord as creator and owner of all creation, applied here as the theological basis for food freedom. Because the Lord (Christ, in Paul's usage) is sovereign over all creation, no created thing is intrinsically polluted. The false dualism that treats the material world as defiled is defeated by the Christological affirmation of creation: the one through whom all things were created (1 Cor 8:6) has not ceded creation's goodness to demonic powers.
10.27: 'If one of the unbelievers invites you to dinner and you are disposed to go, eat whatever is set before you without raising any question on the ground of conscience.' The practical guideline for social interaction with unbelievers extends the creation-goodness principle: food served in an unbeliever's home is not ceremonially problematic. The mission imperative requires social accessibility — Paul's own 'becoming all things to all people' (9:22) includes willingness to eat with unbelievers without scruple, as a sign of the gospel's world-embracing reach.
10.28: 'But if someone says to you, 'This has been offered in sacrifice,' then do not eat it, for the sake of the one who informed you and for the sake of conscience.' The exception: when idol-food's cultic origin is explicitly flagged, the strong believer should decline — not because the food is now spiritually dangerous but because eating in that context, after the cultic association has been made explicit, would signal solidarity with the idol-system to the observer. The other's conscience (whether a weaker believer or a watching unbeliever) governs the strong believer's freedom in Christ.
10.29: 'I do not mean your own conscience, but his. For why should my liberty be determined by someone else's conscience?' The paradox: Paul defends freedom from others' scruples in principle while arguing for voluntary restraint in practice. The freedom Christ won is not freedom from one another but freedom for one another. True Christological freedom is mature enough to voluntarily limit itself for the sake of those whose faith is weaker — the same pattern as Christ who 'though he was rich became poor' (2 Cor 8:9) for those who were spiritually impoverished.
10.30: 'If I partake with thankfulness, why am I denounced because of that for which I give thanks?' The thanksgiving principle — eating with gratitude recognizing God as the giver of food (cf. 1 Tim 4:4-5) — is the norm of Christian eating. The Lord who gave every good gift, and whose gift is acknowledged in thanksgiving, is the one who also determines when that gift's use would harm another. Freedom and thankfulness do not override the love-principle that governs all things in Christ.
10.31: 'So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.' The comprehensive principle: every act, including the most ordinary (eating and drinking), is to be performed in a way that honors God. The glory of God (doxa Theou) is the Christological criterion for all behavior — the same glory that was revealed in Christ's cross (John 12:23-28) and will be fully revealed at the resurrection (ch. 15). Christian freedom is freedom to glorify God in every act, not freedom from accountability to him.
10.32: 'Give no offense to Jews or to Greeks or to the church of God.' The universal mission scope: Jews (the covenant people), Greeks (the Gentile world), and the church (the new community in Christ) must not be caused to stumble by the believer's exercise of freedom. The church of God is the third category alongside ethnic Israel and the Gentile world — a sign that the church is a new creation community transcending ethnic distinctions while remaining in responsibility to all. Paul's example serves the inclusion of all into the family of Christ.
10.33: 'Just as I try to please everyone in everything I do, not seeking my own advantage, but that of many, that they may be saved.' Paul's apostolic principle of maximum adaptability for maximum salvation reach is the conclusion of the entire argument from 9:1. The kenotic pattern — not seeking one's own advantage but the other's — is the Christological form of apostolic mission. 'That they may be saved' is the ultimate Christological horizon: the salvation that Christ won is what apostolic sacrifice serves to extend. The apostle's self-emptying participates in and extends the redemptive logic of the cross.

11.26: A direct revelation: 'For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes.' The Eucharist is not merely commemorative but kerygmatic — every celebration is a proclamation (kataggellō) of the Lord's death. This death-proclamation has an eschatological horizon: 'until he comes' (achris hou elthe). The Lord's Supper situates the community between the cross and the parousia, making every meal a re-entry into the narrative of Christ's saving death and an anticipation of its completion at the return. Christology is embedded in liturgical practice.
11.1: 'Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.' The chain of imitation is explicitly Christological: believers imitate Paul; Paul imitates Christ. The apostle's kenotic mission-pattern (ch. 9), his voluntary limitation of rights, his 'becoming all things to all people' for the gospel — all of these are not original with Paul but derived from the pattern of the incarnate Son who 'came not to be served but to serve' (Mark 10:45). Christological discipleship is mimetic: transformation into the image of Christ by following those who have been transformed.
11.2: 'Now I commend you because you remember me in everything and maintain the traditions even as I delivered them to you.' The apostolic traditions (paradoseis) that Paul delivered are the same vocabulary as the reception-and-transmission of the resurrection tradition (15:3) and the Lord's Supper institution (11:23). These traditions are not Paul's inventions but the deposit of the Lord's own words and actions, transmitted through the apostolic chain. The Christ-community is defined by its fidelity to this transmitted tradition.
11.3: 'The head of every man is Christ, the head of a wife is her husband, and the head of God is Christ.' The Christological claim here is the foundation of the entire head-covering discussion: Christ is the kephalē (head) of every man. Headship in Pauline usage carries the sense of source, authority, and representative relationship — not domination but ordered, loving authority-with-responsibility. That Christ is head of 'every man' (not just believing men) extends his lordship to all humanity, though it is recognized only within the covenant community.
11.4: A man who prays or prophesies with his head covered 'dishonors his head.' The covering of the head that was an act of honor in Roman religious rites (the capite velato posture of the Roman pater familias at sacrifice) is reinterpreted in light of Christ's headship: for a man to veil his head in worship obscures the Christological glory that man as the image of Christ is to display. The creation-order argument that Paul develops in vv. 7-9 is rooted in man's relationship to Christ as his head.
11.5: A woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered 'dishonors her head.' The Corinthian context in which women prophesied (clearly assumed to be legitimate, not prohibited here) is significant: women in the new covenant community participated in vocal, public ministry before the congregation. The head covering for women is a sign of the creation order that contextualizes this Spirit-given ministry within the structure of covenantal relationship — affirming the ministry while situating it within the order Christ has established.
11.6: 'For if a wife will not cover her head, then she should cut her hair short. But since it is disgraceful for a wife to cut her hair short or shave her head, let her cover her head.' The reductio ad absurdum argument: if covering the head is unnecessary, then the cultural marker of femininity (long hair) is equally unnecessary. The logic assumes that the social order communicating gender distinction and proper covenantal relationship is not arbitrary but reflects the created order that Christ's redeeming work restores and fulfills, not obliterates.
11.7: 'For a man ought not to cover his head, since he is the image and glory of God.' Man as the image of God (eikōn kai doxa Theou) is the Genesis 1:26-27 basis for the head-covering argument. Christ, as the true image of God (Col 1:15; 2 Cor 4:4), is the archetype from which Adam's image-bearing derived its meaning. In worship, the man is to display this image-glory uncovered — reflecting the God whose image he bears. The created order is a Christological testimony: it was always pointing toward the one in whose image humanity was made.
11.8: 'For man was not made from woman, but woman from man.' The Genesis 2 creation account — Adam formed first, Eve drawn from Adam — provides the created-order basis for the relational structure Paul is defending. The Christological application: just as woman's origin was from man, the church's origin is from Christ. The structural relationship of source-and-derived is not a hierarchy of value but of covenant order: the one who originates defines the relationship for the one who derives from him.
11.9: 'Neither was man created for woman, but woman for man.' The purposive statement from Genesis 2:18 ('it is not good for the man to be alone; I will make him a helper fit for him') grounds the relational ordering in God's creational purpose. The Christological parallel: the church was created for Christ — to be his body, his bride, his community of glory. Creation's purpose-structures are covenantal: the design reveals the relationship, and both testify to the Christ who was always their goal.
11.10: 'That is why a wife ought to have a symbol of authority on her head, because of the angels.' The mysterious 'because of the angels' (dia tous angelous) has generated extensive interpretation. The most plausible background is the Jewish belief that angels attend worship and observe the order of God's covenant community (cf. 1QSa 2:8-9: the angels present in the assembly; Ps 138:1 LXX). The ordered worship of the church is a performance before cosmic witnesses — the angels who observe whether the community reflects the covenant order established in Christ.
11.11: 'Nevertheless, in the Lord woman is not independent of man nor man of woman.' The qualification is crucial: 'in the Lord' (en kyriō) — the christological sphere — the mutuality and interdependence of man and woman are fully affirmed. The creation-order argument does not establish male superiority but relational structure within mutual dependence. The new community in Christ is not a hierarchy of value but an ordered community of mutual need and mutual gift, where each serves the other's good.
11.12: 'For as woman was made from man, so man is now born of woman. And all things are from God.' The circular mutual dependence — woman from man (creation), man from woman (birth) — and the ultimate divine source ('all things are from God') roots the entire creation-order argument in Christological theology. God, as creator and redeemer, is the source of all relational structure. The one through whom all things were created (8:6) has also ordered the covenantal relationships within which the church worships and reflects his glory.
11.13: 'Judge for yourselves: is it proper for a wife to pray to God with her head uncovered?' The appeal to the community's own judgment about propriety in worship echoes the wisdom appeal of 10:15. The Corinthians who prided themselves on spiritual discernment are invited to exercise that discernment about worship order. The question assumes a normative order of worship in which contextual appropriateness is not arbitrary but reflects the created and covenantal structures that Christ has established and redeemed.
11.14: 'Does not nature itself teach you that if a man wears long hair it is a disgrace for him?' The 'nature' (physis) argument — drawing on observable patterns of creation — was a standard Stoic and Jewish method of moral reasoning from the created order. Paul's use of natural observation alongside scriptural argument reflects the Reformed understanding of general revelation: creation itself testifies to God's design. The Christological underpinning is that creation reflects the creator whose image-structures are being displayed and reflected in ordered worship.
11.15: 'But if a woman has long hair, it is her glory? For her hair is given to her for a covering.' The natural hair-covering given to women as 'glory' (doxa) echoes the 'glory' language of vv. 7-8: man is the glory of God; woman is the glory of man; woman's hair is her glory. The creation gifts that express relational identity — the hair covering given by nature — are not accidental but embedded in the created order. The God who created these natural expressions of relational identity is the same God who establishes covenant worship order in Christ.
11.16: 'If anyone is inclined to be contentious, we have no such custom, nor do the churches of God.' The apostolic appeal to universal church practice is a significant hermeneutical move: the ordering of worship is not a local Pauline preference but the common practice of all the churches of God. The Christological logic: the church universal, formed by the one gospel and the one Spirit, exhibits consistent patterns of worship that reflect the one Lord's ordering of his community. Contentious deviation from this common practice is not freedom but disorder.
11.17: 'But in the following instructions I do not commend you, because when you come together it is not for the better but for the worse.' The Lord's Supper section begins with the most direct rebuke in the letter: the Corinthians' eucharistic practice is actually counter-productive — their gatherings make things worse rather than better. The meal that should be the highest expression of Christ-community (the body of Christ sharing in the body of Christ) has become a site of division, shame, and social hierarchy — the opposite of what the Lord's death proclaimed (v. 26).
11.18: 'For, in the first place, when you come together as a church, I hear that there are divisions among you.' The divisions (schismata) in the Corinthian gathering — the same word as 1:10 — now manifest at the Lord's Supper table, the one place where unity should be most visible. The social stratification of Corinthian society (wealthy members bringing lavish meals, poor members bringing little or nothing) is being replicated at the table of the one who 'though he was rich became poor' for them (2 Cor 8:9). The eucharist is being un-christologized by class distinction.
11.19: 'For there must be factions among you in order that those who are genuine among you may be recognized.' The 'necessity' (dei) of factions echoes apocalyptic language about the eschatological testing that reveals genuine faith. God's providential ordering of even the church's failures serves the purpose of making visible who truly belongs to Christ. The genuine (dokimoi) are revealed through the crisis — those who, even in the disordered Corinthian situation, preserve the eucharistic meaning by waiting for one another and honoring the poor.
11.20: 'When you come together, it is not the Lord's Supper that you eat.' The devastating verdict: what the Corinthians are doing when they 'come together' does not constitute the Lord's Supper. The Lord's Supper requires that the death of the Lord be proclaimed (v. 26) — but a meal where the wealthy eat their fill while the poor go hungry proclaims not the Lord's self-giving but the world's self-keeping. The form of the meal has been evacuated of its Christological content by the social practice surrounding it.
11.21: 'For in eating, each one goes ahead with his own meal. One goes hungry, another gets drunk.' The specific abuse: the wealthy arrived early and ate their privately-brought food (Corinthian practice at communal meals was to bring food proportional to one's status), while the working poor who arrived late found nothing and watched the wealthy drunk. The class-stratified Roman meal custom, imported into the Christian gathering, contradicted the Lord's Supper's essential declaration that the one body of Christ shares in the one sacrifice equally.
11.22: 'What! Do you not have houses to eat and drink in? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing?' Paul's question exposes the theological stakes: treating the poor with contempt at the Lord's table is despising the church of God — the community bought by Christ's blood (6:20; 7:23). The poor in the Corinthian community are members of the body of Christ (12:12-27); shaming them at the eucharist is shaming Christ himself in the members of his body.
11.23: 'For I received from the Lord what I also delivered to you, that the Lord Jesus on the night when he was betrayed took bread.' The institution narrative is introduced as something 'received from the Lord' — apostolic tradition with the highest possible authority. The specific detail 'on the night when he was betrayed' (en tē nykti hē paredideto) grounds the eucharist in the concrete historical moment of the passion. The meal is not a timeless spiritual ritual but a specific memorial of a specific night when the covenant Lord was handed over for the sins of his people.
11.24: 'This is my body which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' The institution of the bread-as-body contains the self-interpretive word of the Lord: 'for you' (hyper hymōn) — the substitutionary/representative character of his death. The 'remembrance' (anamnēsis) is not mere mental recollection but covenantal re-presentation — the memorial that makes present the saving event it recalls, as the Passover Seder re-presented the exodus for each generation. Every eucharist is a participation in the once-for-all sacrifice 'for you.'
11.25: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it, in remembrance of me.' The new covenant (kainē diathēkē) in Christ's blood fulfills and surpasses the Mosaic covenant ratified by the blood of bulls and goats (Exod 24:8). Jeremiah 31:31-34 promised a new covenant with different terms: not written on stone but on hearts; not requiring daily sacrifice but with sins forgiven once-for-all. The cup of the Lord's Supper is the enacted proclamation that this new covenant has been inaugurated in Christ's death — the cup of the new age.
11.27: 'Whoever, therefore, eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord.' The high Christological stakes of eucharistic participation: unworthy eating is not disrespect for a ritual but participation in the guilt of those who crucified the Lord. The body and blood of Christ present at the table make the table a place where the death and resurrection of Christ are enacted — to participate carelessly is to join the ranks of those who dismissed the Son of God. The anamnesis makes the past event contemporaneous with the present community.
11.28: 'Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup.' The self-examination required before eucharistic participation is the Christological discipline of discernment: does this eating-and-drinking proclaim the Lord's death, or does it contradict it? The examination is not perfectionist self-scrutiny but orientation-check: am I approaching this table as one who depends on Christ's body given for me, or am I treating it as a social occasion? The sacrament requires a consistent Christological consciousness.
11.29: 'For anyone who eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on himself.' 'Discerning the body' (diakrinōn to sōma) most likely refers both to the eucharistic body of Christ (present in the bread) and the ecclesial body of Christ (present in the gathered poor who are being shamed). Failing to discern the body of Christ in both its dimensions — the sacramental and the social — brings divine judgment. The Christological unity of the eucharistic and ecclesial body is the ground of Paul's entire argument in this section.
11.30: 'That is why many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.' The physical consequences (weakness, illness, death) are divine judgment on eucharistic abuse. Paul interprets the community's health crises in a Christological frame: the Lord who disciplines those he loves (Heb 12:6; Prov 3:12) visits judgment on those who desecrate the body and blood that are the very means of their salvation. The judgment is not retributive destruction but corrective discipline, as v. 32 will clarify: 'judged by the Lord so that we may not be condemned along with the world.'
11.31: 'But if we judged ourselves truly, we would not be judged.' The self-examination called for in v. 28 is the preventive form of the judgment that God otherwise imposes. The Christological logic: Christ has already borne the ultimate judgment for sin on the cross; the Christian who appropriately applies that judgment to himself ('yes, I am a sinner who needs this sacrifice') is already participating in the cross's verdict and needs no additional judgment. Self-examination is the individual application of the cross's logic.
11.32: 'But when we are judged by the Lord, we are disciplined so that we may not be condemned along with the world.' The distinction between divine discipline and eschatological condemnation is crucial: the illness and death of v. 30 are not signs of divine rejection but of fatherly correction (cf. Heb 12:7-11). The goal of the discipline is inclusion in the final salvation, not exclusion. Christ who bore the condemnation of the world (Rom 8:3) ensures that those who belong to him receive discipline rather than condemnation — even severe discipline serves their ultimate good.
11.33: 'So then, my brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.' The practical Christological application: the cure for the eucharistic disorder is waiting — waiting as the most basic act of mutual regard. The wealthy who arrive early and eat their fill before the poor arrive must wait. Waiting for one another at the Lord's table enacts the Christological principle that all members of the body are equally dependent on the one who died for them. The meal that proclaims the Lord's death must embody the Lord's death in its practice of self-giving.
11.34: 'If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, so that when you come together it will not be for judgment.' The Lord's Supper is not a meal for satisfying hunger — that is what homes are for. The gathering of the church is specifically for the proclamation of the Lord's death (v. 26), for sharing in his body and blood (10:16), and for forming the one body of Christ (10:17). Separating the eucharistic meal from ordinary social dining preserves the Christological meaning of what the church does when it gathers — it gathers around the crucified and risen Lord, not around social status.

15.20: A direct revelation: 'But in fact Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.' The resurrection of Christ is the pivotal fact on which Paul's entire argument turns — not a theological principle but a historical event (Christos egegertai: he has been raised, perfect tense, with present ongoing state). The firstfruits designation makes the resurrection of believers not an optional addendum but the structurally necessary completion of Christ's resurrection: God's harvest of humanity is underway, Christ being the guarantee of the rest. The resurrection hope is not escape from creation but transformation into a new, glorified mode of embodied existence.
15.45: A direct revelation: 'The last Adam became a life-giving spirit.' The Adam-Christ typology here reaches its Christological climax. Adam was the representative head who brought death by disobedience into the created order; Christ is the new representative head who brings life by obedience (and resurrection). The eschatos Adam (last Adam) terminology is unique to Paul: Christ is not merely another Adam in a sequence but the final, definitive human through whom the Adamic story reaches its appointed end. As the life-giving spirit (pneuma zoopoioun), the risen Christ is the source of the Spirit that will transform resurrection-bodies at the last day.
15.57: A direct revelation: 'Thanks be to God who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.' The doxology concludes the resurrection argument: the victory over death, sin, and law (v. 56) is not achieved by believers but given (didonti) by God. The mode of giving is 'through our Lord Jesus Christ' — his resurrection is the mediating event through which God's victory becomes the believer's possession. The personal pronoun 'our' Lord Jesus Christ makes the cosmic Christological triumph intimately personal: this victory is given to those who belong to him.
15.1: Paul declares the gospel (euangelion) he proclaimed is the same gospel the Corinthians received, in which they stand, and through which they are being saved — three stages that describe the gospel's continuing work. The resurrection of Christ is not a detachable appendix to the gospel but its heart.
15.2: Unless you believed in vain — eikē (in vain, without effect) — the conditional tests whether the Corinthians' faith has genuine content. Resurrection-denying faith is not a lesser form of Christianity but no Christianity at all; to deny Christ's resurrection is to believe in a Christ who is still dead.
15.3: Paul hands on as of first importance (en prōtois) what he received — the paradosis (tradition) formula used in 11:23 for the Supper institution; the same authority governs both. Christ died for our sins — the substitutionary logic: his death was for (hyper) the sins of others, not his own.
15.4: He was buried — the burial confirms the reality of death; the resurrection on the third day according to the scriptures fulfills the Jonah pattern (Matt 12:40), Hosea 6:2 (after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up), and Psalm 16:10 (you will not abandon my soul to Sheol).
15.5: He appeared to Cephas, then to the Twelve — the primary eyewitness testimony. Peter's personal encounter with the risen Christ transformed the one who denied him three times into the one who preached him to thousands at Pentecost. The appearance to the Twelve constituted them as the reconstituted Israel of the new age.
15.6: He appeared to more than five hundred brothers at one time — the mass appearance whose witnesses are still mostly living when Paul writes. The resurrection is not a private mystical experience but a public, verifiable event with hundreds of living witnesses.
15.7: He appeared to James — the Lord's own brother, who was not a disciple during Jesus's ministry (John 7:5: not even his brothers believed in him). The appearance to James is the catalyst for his conversion and subsequent leadership of the Jerusalem church (Gal 1:19; Acts 15:13). The resurrection overcame the resistance of Jesus's own household.
15.8: Last of all, as to one untimely born, he appeared also to me — Paul's encounter on the Damascus road (Acts 9:3-9) is the last in the series of post-resurrection appearances. The risen Christ commissioned Paul as he commissioned the Twelve, claiming Paul as his apostle despite Paul's prior persecution of the church.
15.9: I am the least of the apostles, not worthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God — the cross of Christ transforms the persecutor into the preacher. The grace that reached Paul on the Damascus road is the same grace that the Corinthians received. The apostle's unworthiness magnifies the grace.
15.10: But by the grace of God I am what I am — the resurrection-encounter with Christ did not merely give Paul information; it constituted his identity. The grace (charis) that defines him is the grace of the risen Christ who appeared to him and sent him.
15.11: Whether then it was I or they, so we preach and so you believed — the unity of the apostolic witness across all the resurrection appearances guarantees the content of the gospel. The risen Christ is the same Christ regardless of which apostle encountered him.
15.12: Now if Christ is proclaimed as raised from the dead, how can some among you say there is no resurrection of the dead? — the logical incoherence Paul exposes: to deny resurrection in general is to deny Christ's resurrection in particular, which is to deny the entire foundation of the gospel they received.
15.13: If there is no resurrection of the dead, then Christ has not been raised — the argument runs backward from the general to the particular. Christ's resurrection is not an exception to a general rule about the dead; it is the first instance of the general resurrection that will include all who are in him.
15.14: If Christ has not been raised, then our proclamation is empty and your faith is empty — the cross without the resurrection is a tragedy, not a gospel. The empty tomb is what makes the cross redemptive rather than merely cruel; the risen Christ is the proof that his death achieved what it claimed.
15.15: We are even found to be misrepresenting God — if Christ was not raised, the apostles have been claiming that God did something he did not do. The integrity of the apostolic witness stands or falls with the resurrection. A Christianity without the risen Christ is built on false testimony about God.
15.16: For if the dead are not raised, not even Christ has been raised — the repetition for emphasis: Paul cannot let the Corinthians find a middle position. They cannot accept Christ's resurrection while denying the resurrection of the dead; the two stand or fall together.
15.17: If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile (mataia) and you are still in your sins — the atonement itself is validated by the resurrection. Christ was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification (Rom 4:25); the resurrection is the Father's declaration that the Son's atoning work was accepted.
15.18: Then also those who have fallen asleep in Christ have perished — the full weight of the argument arrives: if there is no resurrection, Christian martyrs and all who died trusting in Christ are simply gone. The hope that sustained them was false. Paul forces this conclusion so the Corinthians feel the full horror of their position.
15.19: If in Christ we have hope only for this life, we are of all people most to be pitied — the apostolic life of suffering (vv.30-32) makes sense only in light of resurrection. A suffering servant of a dead Christ would be the most pathetic figure imaginable. But the resurrection makes Paul's sufferings the birth-pangs of new creation.
15.21: For as by a man came death, by a man has also come the resurrection of the dead — the Adam/Christ typology: the scope of Christ's redemptive work matches the scope of Adam's fall. A man introduced death into the human family; a man has introduced resurrection life. The symmetry is precise.
15.22: As in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive — the two humanities, the two federal heads, the two histories. Union with Adam results in death; union with Christ results in resurrection. The all in each case is bounded by the respective union: all who are in Adam die; all who are in Christ will be raised.
15.23: Christ the firstfruits (aparche), then at his coming those who belong to Christ — the eschatological sequence: Christ's resurrection is the first sheaf of the harvest, the pledge of the full harvest to come. The parousia (his coming) is when the harvest is completed — when those who are his are raised at his return.
15.24: Then comes the end, when he delivers the kingdom to God the Father after destroying every rule and every authority and power — the eschatological programme: Christ's reign is purposefully aimed at the elimination of every force that opposes God's rule. The handing over of the kingdom to the Father is the completion of the Son's mediatorial mission.
15.25: For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet — Psalm 110:1 cited: the Lord's word to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool. Christ's current reign at the Father's right hand (Acts 2:34-35; Heb 1:13) is already the conquest of his enemies in progress.
15.26: The last enemy to be destroyed is death — death personified as the final hostile power. Christ's resurrection has already struck the decisive blow; the final resurrection will complete death's defeat. Revelation 20:14 calls death's end the second death — death itself will die.
15.27: God has put all things in subjection under his feet — Psalm 8:6 read christologically: the human figure of Psalm 8 (what is man?) receives cosmic dominion. The writer of Hebrews makes the same move (Heb 2:5-9): we do not yet see everything in subjection, but we see Jesus, crowned with glory and honor.
15.28: When all things are subjected to him, then the Son himself will also be subjected to him who put all things in subjection under him, so that God may be all in all — the telos of redemption: the Son's eternal submission to the Father is not ontological subordination but the completion of the mission. The entire created order is brought into the fullness of God's life.
15.29: Otherwise, what do people mean by being baptized on behalf of the dead? — Paul uses this practice as an ad hominem argument without endorsing it: if there is no resurrection, even those who practice proxy baptism for the dead are acting on a false hope. The practice proves that even the Corinthians believed in bodily resurrection in some form.
15.30: Why are we in danger every hour? — Paul's own apostolic suffering serves as an argument from experience: if there is no resurrection, his readiness to face death at every turn is not courage but stupidity.
15.31: I die every day — the daily dying of the apostolic life (2 Cor 4:10-11: always carrying in the body the dying of Jesus) makes sense only if resurrection is real. The cross-pattern that Paul embodies presupposes the resurrection-pattern that follows.
15.32: What do I gain if, humanly speaking, I fought with beasts at Ephesus? If the dead are not raised, let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die — Paul offers the Epicurean alternative in its starkest form. If the resurrection is false, Epicurean pleasure-maximization is the rational response. But Paul's life choices prove he does not believe the resurrection is false.
15.33: Do not be deceived: bad company ruins good morals — the resurrection-denying teaching circulating in Corinth is not merely wrong; it is morally corrupting. False theology produces bad ethics (the connection runs throughout the letter: what you believe about Christ determines how you treat your body, your neighbor, your community).
15.34: Wake up from your drunken stupor and do not go on sinning — the command to sober up (eknepsate) addresses the moral consequences of resurrection-denial. Those who deny the resurrection have no ground for the ethic of self-sacrifice, neighbor-love, or endurance that the gospel requires.
15.35: How are the dead raised? With what kind of body do they come? — the fool's question that Paul immediately answers with the seed analogy. The question assumes that resurrection body must be identical to burial body, which Paul rejects. The risen Christ is the prototype: recognizable yet transformed, physical yet imperishable.
15.36: What you sow does not come to life unless it dies — the seed-death-new-life pattern is the resurrection pattern in nature. Jesus used the same analogy: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit (John 12:24). The seed's death is the condition of the plant's life.
15.37: What you sow is not the body that is to be, but a bare kernel — the continuity between the sown seed and the risen plant is real but the form is transformed. So with the resurrection body: continuous identity with the buried body, but radically transformed in mode of existence.
15.38: God gives it a body as he has chosen — the resurrection body is God's creative act, not the reconstitution of existing material. The diversity of bodies in creation (v.39-41) shows that God has no single template; the resurrection body will be the appropriate form for immortal, Spirit-animated existence.
15.39: Not all flesh is the same flesh — the diversity of created bodies prepares for the resurrection body's difference from the present body. The Creator who made the variety of flesh in creation can create a body appropriate for the resurrection age.
15.40: There are heavenly bodies and earthly bodies — the contrast between celestial bodies (stars, with their radiant glory) and terrestrial bodies prepares for the contrast between the present mortal body and the resurrection body. Each has its appropriate doxa (glory/radiance).
15.41: Star differs from star in glory — the diversity of glory even within a single category (stars) suggests that the resurrection body's glory will be qualitatively different from, not merely more of, the present body's.
15.42: So is it with the resurrection of the dead — the analogy applied: sown in perishability, raised in imperishability. The four antitheses in vv.42-44 describe the transformation that the resurrection of Christ has inaugurated and that believers will share at the parousia.
15.43: Sown in dishonor, raised in glory; sown in weakness, raised in power — the cross of Christ maps onto the seed pattern: crucified in weakness, raised in power (2 Cor 13:4); crucified in dishonor, raised in glory (Phil 2:9-11). The believer's resurrection participates in Christ's own transformation.
15.44: Sown a natural body, raised a spiritual body — the psychikon soma (body animated by natural life) gives way to the pneumatikon soma (body animated by the Spirit). The risen Christ is the prototype: his resurrection body was the same body that was buried, yet transformed into the life of the Spirit.
15.46: The spiritual is not first but the natural and then the spiritual — the sequence of the ages: present age (psychikon), then age to come (pneumatikon). The incarnation follows this sequence: the eternal Son took on the psychikon existence of Adam before being raised in the pneumatikon existence of the last Adam.
15.47: The first man is from the earth, earthy; the second man is from heaven — the origin of the two Adams: the first Adam was formed from the dust (Gen 2:7); the second Adam is from heaven (ho deuteros anthrōpos ex ouranou) — the incarnate Son who descended from the Father and will return to draw his own to himself (John 14:3).
15.48: As was the man of dust, so also are those who are of the dust; as is the man of heaven, so also are those who are of heaven — the solidarity of the two humanities: those in Adam bear his image (mortal, earthy); those in Christ will bear his image (imperishable, heavenly). The transformation is not yet complete but is guaranteed by union with the risen Lord.
15.49: Just as we have borne the image of the man of dust, we shall also bear the image of the man of heaven — the eschatological promise: those united with Christ will be conformed to his image (Rom 8:29: predestined to be conformed to the image of his Son). The transformation begun in regeneration will be completed in resurrection.
15.50: Flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God — the present mortal constitution is not the vehicle for the kingdom's ultimate form. Transformation is required, not merely survival. The kingdom of God that Jesus proclaimed as present in his ministry will be consummated in the age when death itself has been destroyed.
15.51: Behold, I tell you a mystery — Paul introduces a previously unrevealed truth: not all will die before the parousia. We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed — the mystery is the transformation of the living at Christ's return, which Paul also describes in 1 Thessalonians 4:15-17.
15.52: In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet — the eschatological trumpet (Matt 24:31; 1 Thess 4:16; Rev 11:15) signals Christ's return and the completion of his reign. The instantaneous transformation (atomos, an indivisible moment) underscores that resurrection is divine act, not biological process.
15.53: This perishable body must put on the imperishable, and this mortal body must put on immortality — the clothing metaphor (ependysasthai) echoes Paul's language in 2 Cor 5:2-4 and anticipates the new-creation imagery of Revelation 7:9-14. The resurrection body is not the destruction of the old but the clothing of it with incorruption.
15.54: Death is swallowed up in victory — citing Isaiah 25:8 (God will swallow up death forever; he will wipe away tears from all faces). The banquet of Isaiah 25 on the holy mountain, where God destroys death, is the eschatological feast that the Lord's Supper anticipates. Christ's resurrection is the first victory in the war that ends with death's complete defeat.
15.55: O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting? — citing Hosea 13:14 (LXX). Paul applies to the general resurrection what Hosea spoke of in a context of divine judgment and restoration. The taunting of death — an appropriate response from those who have seen Christ raised — anticipates the complete victory that is certain.
15.56: The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law — the cross addressed all three: Christ bore sin, fulfilled the law, and defeated death. The three are intertwined: the law defines sin; sin empowers death; death is defeated by Christ who bore both sin and law's curse in our place (Gal 3:13).
15.58: Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain — the resurrection grounds the ethics: because Christ is raised, nothing done in the Lord's name is ultimately empty. The resurrection makes every act of love, every moment of faithful labor, permanently significant.

2.1: Paul's arrival without eloquence or superior wisdom embodies the cross-pattern: the message about Christ crucified is delivered in a manner consistent with its content. A polished rhetorical performance would contradict the cross's logic — the medium of proclamation must be shaped by the crucified and weakened Messiah it proclaims. Apostolic self-presentation is Christologically formed.
2.2: The decision to know nothing except Jesus Christ and him crucified is a Christological focusing that governs all of Paul's ministry in Corinth. The entire letter is the unpacking of this single commitment: every problem Paul addresses (factions, sexual ethics, idol food, gifts, resurrection) is answered from the cross as the center. Christ crucified is not one topic among many but the interpretive key to all topics.
2.3: Weakness, fear, and trembling: Paul's personal manner conforms to the cross-pattern. The crucified Christ died in weakness (2 Cor 13:4); the apostle proclaims him in weakness. There is a Christological coherence between the content of the message and the condition of the messenger — both reveal God's power through human fragility. This is not incidental but structural: the weakness of the messenger prevents faith from resting on human charisma.
2.4: The demonstration of the Spirit and power: the validation of the crucified Christ's message comes not through argumentative skill but through the Spirit's accompanying power — healings, transformed lives, the tangible presence of the risen Christ working through the proclamation. This Spirit-power is the resurrection's ongoing expression: Christ risen is active in the proclamation about Christ crucified.
2.5: Faith resting on God's power, not human wisdom: Christological faith is necessarily faith in the one who acts, not in the persuasive force of an argument. The shift from argument-based confidence to power-based confidence is a shift from trust in human capacity to trust in the risen Christ who authenticates his own gospel through the Spirit.
2.6: The wisdom among the mature is the wisdom of God's plan in Christ, hidden through the ages and now revealed through the Spirit. This is not a second-tier Christianity for advanced practitioners but the full depth of what the cross means — the disclosure of God's eternal strategy for the salvation of the world through the apparent foolishness of the crucified Christ. Maturity in Christ means understanding the cross more deeply, not transcending it.
2.7: The mystery hidden before the ages but destined for our glory: Paul's mystērion is not a secret doctrine for insiders but God's hidden plan now revealed in Christ. The mystery is Christ himself (Col 1:27: the mystery is Christ in you, the hope of glory). Predestined pro tōn aiōnōn (before the ages): the cross was not God's emergency contingency plan but his eternal intention for the world's salvation.
2.8: The rulers of this age crucified the Lord of glory: the cross is the moment of maximum Christological revelation and maximum human misreading. Christ is the Lord of glory (ho Kyrios tēs doxēs) — the title applied to YHWH in Ps 24:7-10 — yet he was crucified by those who could not recognize what they were doing. The cross is simultaneously the ultimate expression of divine glory and the ultimate human rejection of divine wisdom. Had the powers known, they would not have crucified him — but their ignorance was overruled by God's purpose.
2.9: What eye has not seen, ear has not heard, heart has not conceived — these are the things God has prepared for those who love him. The content of this eschatological preparation is revealed in and through Christ: the resurrection, the new creation, the fullness of God's presence. All the unimagined realities that await believers are guaranteed by Christ's own resurrection as firstfruits (15:20). The quotation gestures toward the fullness that the cross opens up.
2.10: God revealed these things through his Spirit, who searches the deep things of God. The Spirit's role is to mediate the knowledge of Christ: what the Father knows about the Son, the Spirit discloses to believers. The deep things of God (ta bathe tou Theou) are the Christological mysteries that the Spirit alone can make known — the meaning of the cross, the identity of the crucified Lord, the eschatological glory that awaits.
2.11: No one knows the things of God except the Spirit of God: the epistemological principle that grounds Christological knowledge in Spirit-disclosure. To know Christ truly — to understand why the cross is wisdom and not foolishness — requires the Spirit who knows the Father's purposes from the inside. The cross is not self-evidently wise; it requires the Spirit's illuminating work to be seen as God's power and wisdom.
2.12: We received the Spirit from God so that we might know the things graciously given us by God: the Spirit mediates the knowledge of Christ's gifts. The things given by God (ta hypo tou Theou charisthenta) are specifically the Christological gifts: righteousness, sanctification, redemption (1:30). The Spirit's work is to make the community aware of what it already possesses in Christ.
2.13: Paul speaks in Spirit-taught words rather than human-wisdom words: the form of apostolic proclamation is Christologically and pneumatically shaped. Expressing spiritual things in spiritual words means that the vocabulary and categories of the gospel are given by the Spirit, not constructed by human rhetoric. The language of the cross is taught by the crucified one's own Spirit.
2.14: The unspiritual person cannot receive the things of the Spirit: the epistemological barrier is not intellectual but relational.
